checks.moreThanSixteenLines: You used more than sixteen lines of code.

plings.name: Plings
plings.description: Start off by getting the Pling sounds right. If the number is divisible by 3, return "Pling".

plangs.name: Plangs
plangs.description: Now get the Plang sounds right. If the number is divisible by 5, add "Plang" to the result. If it's divisible by both 3 and 5, return "PlingPlang".

plongs.name: Plongs
plongs.description: Now get the Plong sounds right. If the number is divisible by 7, add "Plong" to the result. Numbers can be divisible by multiple factors.

noSound.name: Numbers with no raindrop sound
noSound.description: Finally, if the number is not divisible by 3, 5, or 7, return the number itself as a string.

solveInSixteenLines.name: Solve in sixteen lines of code
solveInSixteenLines.description: Can you solve this with only sixteen lines of code?

bonus1.name: Sixteen lines of code
bonus1.description: Solve the exercise with only sixteen lines of code.

onlyOneSound.question: Why does my function only return one sound?
onlyOneSound.answer: If you `return` inside each `if`, you give up as soon as the first divisor matches. You want to combine them. Instead of returning, append the sound to a result string and keep going.

buildCombinedSound.question: How do I build up the combined sound?
buildCombinedSound.answer: Start with an empty result string. For each divisor (3, 5, 7), if it divides the number, append the corresponding sound onto the end of the result using concatenation with `+`.

noneMatch.question: What if none of the divisors match?
noneMatch.answer: If the result is still empty after all three checks, the answer is just the number itself as a string. Convert the number and return that.

convertToString.question: I return the number but it wants me to return it as a string. How do I do that?
convertToString.answer: Numbers and strings are different types, even if they look the same. You can convert a number to a string by putting it inside a template string with `${...}`.

howManyReturns.question: How many return statements should I have?
howManyReturns.answer: Just one or two, typically. The whole point is letting the if-statements modify the result string, then returning that result once at the end.
